27.1.label: Question 27.1
27.1.question:
Compare the three different approaches used to underwrite private medical insurance contracts
   from the points of view of the insurer and the policyholder.

Solution: The three different approaches used to underwrite private medical insurance are: full medical
underwriting, moratorium underwriting and medical history disregarded (MHD).
Cost: Full medical underwriting is the most onerous form of underwriting and as such is the most costly
This is because all applications will be medically underwritten at the outset.                      [½]
Moratorium underwriting is less costly, since policies will only be underwritten if claims are made
However, underwriting at the claim stage may be relatively complicated and time‐consuming. [½]
MHD will be the least costly, because there is no formal underwriting, either at policy inception,
The more attractive the cover (see below), the higher the likely volumes of business, which could
lead to economies of scale and hence expense savings for the insurer.                          [½]
Anti\u2010selection risk: Since full medical underwriting gives the insurer the most information about the individual’s
medical risk, it is the most likely method to protect the insurer against anti‐selection, ...       [½]
... and lead to the most appropriate terms being set for each applicant.                            [½]
Anti‐selection is tackled to some extent with moratorium underwriting, since claims for
pre‐existing conditions are excluded in the moratorium period.                                      [½]
However, policyholders may still be able to select against the insurer if they are aware of pre‐
existing conditions when insurance is purchased, but there is no written evidence for them.      [½]
MHD is open to high levels of anti‐selection.                                                       [½]
Level of premiums: Premiums will be raised where anti‐selection risk is increased …                                    [½]
… but reduced where expenses are lower for certain levels of underwriting.                          [½]
MHD premiums are likely to be the highest due to the potential for anti‐selection (which are likely
to far outweigh the cost savings of low/no underwriting).                                       [½]
Full medical underwriting should lead to the lowest anti‐selection risk. Although underwriting
expenses will be high, premiums are likely to be relatively low for this form of underwriting. [½]
Premiums under moratorium underwriting w: the shorter the period, the higher the anti‐selection risk, and so the higher the premium. [½]
Speed of application: Full medical underwriting is the most detailed, and hence time‐consuming, form of underwriting,
which may be unattractive to applicants who want to get immediate cover.                     [½]
By contrast, moratorium underwriting and MHD will enable the applicant to obtain immediate
Attractiveness of cover: Full medical underwriting may result in certain conditions being excluded permanently, which
may make cover unattractive to applicants.                                                   [½]
However the certainty associated with knowing exactly what is covered from the outset may
The exclusion of certain conditions for a temporary period, under moratorium underwriting, may
not necessarily make the contract unattractive …                                            [½]
… eg it may be attractive to genuine applicants: those who are not just trying to select against the
insurer by purchasing cover in order to make an immediate claim.                                  [½]
However, moratorium underwriting may be unattractive due to the uncertainty of what is
covered in the first few years.                                                                               [½]
The cover is likely to be offered subject to a blanket exclusion on pre‐existing conditions, and only
the most medically aware policyholders are likely to understand the connectivity of medical
MHD will cover all conditions, which will be attractive to applicants – particularly those in poor

27.10.label: Question 27.10
27.10.scenario:
You are the actuary in charge of individual income protection business. A number of your medical
 underwriters will be retiring shortly. Replacement staff must be trained to take their place and
you have been asked to produce a short presentation on underwriting.
27.10.question:
List the considerations that would be taken into account when underwriting for income
protection insurance.

Medical evidence:          the proposal form (eg own medical history, family medical history)
Lifestyle information:          details of the applicant’s leisure pursuits                                                [½]
Other:          any regulatory or social constraints (eg restricting the use of genetic test results)    [½]

27.11.label: Question 27.11
27.11.scenario:
An insurer issues private medical insurance policies both to individuals and as group schemes to

employers. All applicants for individual policies are medically underwritten at inception, and
those that are accepted for insurance are charged the standard rate of premium for the policy
applied for.

The insurer’s product development manager has proposed altering the acceptance procedures,
for all new applicants for individual policies. His proposal is to replace the current initial
underwriting with a moratorium approach.

(i).question:
Describe the moratorium approach and discuss the advantages and disadvantages of the
manager’s proposal.                                                                [7]

One of the insurer’s underwriters has pointed out that neither the existing nor the proposed
acceptance methods deals satisfactorily, from the policyholders’ point of view, with any
pre‐existing conditions that the policyholder may have at the time of application.

Moratorium approach: All applicants will be automatically given cover at the standard rate of premium.                    [½]
No underwriting questions have to be answered prior to acceptance.                                   [½]
Instead of initial underwriting, the insurer will impose a blanket exclusion on claims for treatment
for, or related to, any pre‐existing medical conditions that the policyholder has at the time the
Pre‐existing conditions will usually be defined as those for which the policyholder received
treatment during a period (eg five years) prior to starting cover.                                   [½]
The exclusions apply to any treatment for these, or any related, conditions that occur within a
moratorium period (typically two or three years) of starting cover.                             [½]
The moratorium period begins at policy inception and, during this period, the policyholder must
receive no treatment for such conditions in order for subsequent claims for these conditions to be
However, if the policy does receive treatment for such conditions during this period, the
moratorium period restarts. So, in effect, the policyholder must not receive treatment for the
condition for a continuous period of time that is at least as long as the moratorium period.   [½]
The insurer has to gather information about the policyholder’s medical history at the time of any
claim occurring within the moratorium period.                                                   [½]
Advantages and disadvantages: +         It should save on insurer’s costs, as it only needs to gather underwriting information for
policyholders who claim during the moratorium period.                                     [½]
+         Lower costs should bring down premiums, making them cheaper for policyholders, and
thereby increase sales and profit.                                                 [½]
+         No initial underwriting leads to less hassle (for both seller and customer) at entry, ...         [½]
... and so will make the policy more attractive and easier to sell.                               [½]
–         It may be difficult to gather the necessary information, retrospectively, at the time of
claim.                                                                                   [½]
–         Any pre‐existing conditions that go unnoticed as a result could cause losses for the
insurer.                                                                                          [½]
–         Policyholders may find it difficult to understand from what treatments they are excluded,
as they may not be aware of all the links and associations between the different medical
conditions.                                                                            [1]
–         The pre‐existing conditions may be such that some policyholders would be very unlikely
ever to receive any benefit under their policies.                                     [½]
–         Where policyholders are unclear about which conditions are excluded, there is a risk that
claims will be made for treatments that end up being rejected.                         [½]
There is therefore an increased risk of upsetting policyholders, and damaging the
reputation of the insurer.                                                                        [½]
–         The insurer may find practical difficulties in managing policies that were issued before the
proposed change, at the same time as managing the new policies.                           [½]
[Maximum 7]

(ii).question:
Explain the problem alluded to above, and describe an alternative method of acceptance
that could address this concern.                                                     [3]

Employees who join one of the insurer’s group schemes are neither medically underwritten, nor
subject to a moratorium approach.

Dealing differently with pre\u2010existi: Under either method, a policyholder with pre‐existing conditions will not be covered for future
treatment for those conditions, at least for the duration of the moratorium period.             [½]
An alternative is full initial medical underwriting, where premiums can be loaded to take into
account the increased risk of claims from pre‐existing conditions.                             [½]
This means that policyholders can get insurance cover for these conditions, though at a cost.               [½]
However, if the condition is too onerous, ie such that the insurer could not quantify an additional
premium that could adequately cover the risk, then this could not be accepted for cover.         [½]
The policyholder would then have to accept an exclusion for the condition, or not take up the
Alternatively, the insurer may defer acceptance of the policy temporarily, because changes to the
applicant’s condition may enable the risk to be adequately quantified at a future date.        [½]

(iii).question:
Explain the circumstances under which this might be feasible without producing heavy
losses for the insurer.

Why group employees have no underwriting: This is probably only likely to be possible if membership of the scheme is compulsory for all
… or if the scheme were non‐contributory and take‐up was very high …                                  [½]
… or if employees could only choose to join the scheme at or shortly after starting employment.
It would probably not be possible if employees were given a free choice of whether or when to
join, because this could lead to significant anti‐selection, particularly where employees are making
This is because only those employees who see an imminent need for treatment are likely to join.
The need for underwriting is also less for group schemes that are experience‐rated or self‐funded
(and/or may participate in profit‐sharing with the insurer).                                   [½]
Employers are therefore incentivised to keep claim costs down to reasonable levels, as high claims
would feed back to them through higher premiums (or less profit share).                         [½]

27.12.label: Question 27.12
27.12.scenario:
An insurance company has sold IP contracts for a number of years. Over recent years, there has

been a significant increase in the number of applicants that are considered to be substandard
risks and are subsequently offered special terms.

(i).question:
List the ways in which the insurance company can apply special terms to an IP contract,
and discuss which might be the most appropriate for an applicant with a pre‐existing
mental health condition.                                                                [9]

The insurance company has decided to apply exclusions to applicants with pre‐existing mental
health conditions. The marketing department has suggested that this is likely to make the terms
uncompetitive and has suggested applying a premium reduction in addition to the exclusion.

The special terms that might be offered:          postponement of a decision to a later date                                                  [½]
         a different type of contract, eg a short‐term accident and sickness contract.               [½]
Alternatively, the insurance company mig:          accept the risk on standard terms                                                           [½]
         offer the risk to a reinsurer facultatively with zero retention                             [½]
         decline the applicant.                                                                      [½]
Applicants with pre‐existing mental health conditions may accept that they pose a higher
insurance risk and may therefore accept a higher premium ...                                          [½]
... however this might make the cover unaffordable.                                                   [½]
A higher premium may be preferable to a lower benefit, as applicants are likely to have assessed
their income needs and have applied for an annual benefit level to meet those needs.           [½]
An exclusion clause, which excludes claims resulting from the pre‐existing mental health
condition, may be acceptable to the applicant ...                                                        [½]
... however this may have been the applicant’s main reason for applying for the policy, and as a
result, the cover might not meet needs.                                                          [½]
It may also be difficult to prove whether a claim is a result of the mental health condition (possibly
indirectly).                                                                                       [½]
Postponement of a decision to a later date is unlikely to be appropriate, as mental health
problems are often long‐term, possibly recurring, conditions ...                                         [½]
... however, if the mental health issues are the result of a recent event, eg depression resulting
from a recent death of a relative, then postponing the decision might enable the insurance
company to ascertain whether the problem is likely to recur in the future.                         [½]
Offering the applicant a different type of contract, eg a short‐term accident and sickness contract,
might be appropriate depending on the applicant’s individual circumstances.                      [½]
For example, applicants may suffer from relatively short bouts of mental health incidences, in
which case a short‐term policy might meet their needs.                                         [½]
It also depends on other factors, such as the level of employer / State benefits available and the
applicant’s level of savings.                                                                     [½]
Offering the applicant standard terms is unlikely to be appropriate, although this depends on the
extent to which the insurance company deems the applicant to be a higher risk, which in turn
depends on:                                                                                     [½]
         the occupation of the applicant                                                                [¼]
         the severity of the existing mental health condition.                                          [¼]
Reinsuring 100% of the risk on a facultative basis is likely to be appropriate for very unusual risks.
This is unlikely to be the case here as there may be a number of applicants (or potential
applicants) who have suffered from mental health issues.                                            [½]
Declining the applicant may be the most appropriate option if the risk is deemed to be
unacceptably high ...                                                                                    [½]
... however, this might lead to bad publicity for the insurance company.                                 [½]
There may be regulation / legislation on the course of action that is permitted in certain cases. [½]
[Maximum 9]

Appropriateness of also applying a premi: The rationale behind the marketing department’s suggestion is that the insurance company is
offering reduced coverage, ie it is not providing cover for claims resulting from mental health
problems, and so it should reduce the premium to reflect this.                                  [½]
The premium reduction might be calculated as the probability of making a mental health claim
multiplied by the expected cost of a mental health claim.                                    [½]
This should increase the competitiveness of the terms of the contract for those with pre‐existing
mental health conditions, which may increase business volumes.                                  [½]
However, the insurance company should consider the claims experience of these risks as a whole,
rather than considering the mental health claims in isolation.                              [½]
This is because mental health condition may affect the incidence and duration of claims from
other causes. In particular:                                                                 [½]
         There may be an impact on claim incidence rates from other causes.                          [½]
For example, depression is likely to increase the incidence of other illnesses, eg heart
disease, back pain.                                                                      [½]
         There may be an impact on claim durations from other causes.                                [½]
For example, consider a claimant suffering from both back pain and depression. Back pain
may be eased by exercising and strengthening the relevant muscles, however claimants
who are also depressed may be less likely to carry out such exercises, thus leading to
slower recovery and hence longer claims.                                               [½]
         There may be an impact on the severity of the mental health condition as a result of other
(valid) claims. This might increase the risk of longer and/or more frequent future (valid)
claims.                                                                                  [½]
For example, a claimant with back pain may suffer from anxiety or distress as a result of
the back pain, hence worsening the mental health condition. This may then worsen the
back pain (as above).                                                                   [½]
         There may be an increased risk of fraudulent claims, ie if policyholders cannot claim for
the excluded conditions, they might try to claim under a different condition.             [½]
This is relatively unlikely for a mental health condition.                             [½]
[Maximum 5]

27.13.label: Question 27.13
27.13.scenario:
A long‐term health and care insurer that sells only critical illness policies has been losing business
 recently to its competitors.
27.13.question: State ways in which the insurer could remedy its competitive position.

Solution: The insurer could reduce its premium rates for new business.                                           [½]
Alternatively, for the same premium, it could enhance the benefits for new business, for example
by increasing the benefit amount or benefit escalation rate.                                  [½]
The events for which a valid claim may be made could be widened.                                      [½]
The insurer could add options to the policy (ideally at no or minimal cost), eg an option to renew
the policy at the end of the contract period on non‐guaranteed terms.                            [½]
Guarantees could be added, eg a guarantee that premiums (if reviewable) would not increase
beyond a certain percentage.                                                               [½]
The insurer could increase salaries / commissions payable to the sellers of the product to improve
the desire to sell the business ...                                                             [½]
… or it could start selling via a new channel, such as the internet.                                  [½]
The insurer could launch a new CI product, for example, with guaranteed terms and premiums
where only reviewable products have been sold to date …                                    [½]
… or with simplified benefits at a more attractive price.                                              [½]
Offering alternative products will give potential customers and sellers more choice.                   [½]
The insurer might want to increase awareness for itself or its product in the market, by increasing
the advertising for the brand or the product.                                                    [½]
Incentives such as vouchers, clocks etc, could be offered to new customers in order to increase
sales.                                                                                          [½]
The policy terms and conditions could be made more favourable, for example by decreasing the
survival period applicable.                                                                 [½]
The underwriting could be made simpler, eg less stringent criteria or asking fewer questions.
More policyholders will be accepted, making the product more attractive.                               [½]
The service offered to customers could be improved, ...                                                [½]
... for example by investing in customer care at the policy issue stage (eg better documentation)
or by improving the claims process.                                                             [½]
[Maximum 6]
Corporate bonds should offer a higher expected return than government bonds of equal term to
reflect default risk and lower marketability.
Security could be a significant problem, especially if the issuing company has a low credit rating.
The value of the bonds may fluctuate with the markets, but this will not be too important if the
investor is prepared to hold the bonds to redemption.
Like equities, property normally has a relatively high expected return. The rental income typically
gives a low income yield, although this should eventually increase in real terms.
Property investment is normally seen as secure, however, the rental income may suffer occasional
interruptions and the market value can vary significantly over the long term.
It is a very illiquid investment, with significant dealing and management / maintenance expenses.
It is a very long‐term investment. As with equities, it can be thought of as a perpetuity, however,
unlike with equities, buying and then selling quickly is not practical due to high dealing costs.
Direct property investment can only be made in large chunks – so a small investor may have
problems with diversification.
(i)       Why perfect matching isn’t usually desirable
Index‐linked bonds may go some way to matching the liability, although claims costs might inflate
at a greater speed than the index. If this is the case, then other asset classes that traditionally
give higher long‐term real returns may be more suitable, such as equities and property. However,
the volatility of such assets needs to be borne in mind.
The insurer will bear the loss, since the liabilities in respect of the unit‐linked policies will increase
in line with the other higher‐performing assets.
This is the reason why such mis‐matching is not common: the risk of a sizeable loss is too great,
even if the free assets are sufficient to prevent the loss causing sudden insolvency.
A final point to note in this section is the consideration as to the appropriate matching strategy
for the free assets themselves. In effect they do back liabilities: they form part of the capital of
the company, so there is an obligation to provide a return to the providers of that capital. Hence
one would expect investment in high yielding assets to help meet this obligation.
