Syllabus objective: 3.5       Demonstrate the application of asset‐liability matching as a risk management
3.5.1 State the principles of investment and how they apply to health and care
3.5.2 Analyse health and care insurance liabilities into different types for
3.5.3 Propose an appropriate asset‐liability matching strategy for different types of
0 Introduction: This chapter covers how a health and care insurance company should invest its assets. The
investment strategy followed by the insurer can be of considerable importance to its commercial
However, it is important to realise that most health products are protection products and
therefore tend to have low reserves. For these products, investment returns will be far less
Question: Name one healthcare product where investment returns are of considerable importance.
(i)       Why isn’t perfect matching usually desirable?
(ii)      Under what circumstances would perfect matching be desirable?
Give an example of a health and care insurance product that typically has benefit payments of
each of the four types listed above.
How good a match for liabilities linked to a price index would equities be?
In the event of the return on the chosen assets being lower than that of those determining the
unit price, who bears the loss?
Pre\u2010funded long\u2010term care, whe: Where reserves are significant enough, high returns on assets will help to provide good financial
results, which can benefit the shareholders (through higher profits) and/or policyholders (through
lower premiums). There will also be a benefit to unit‐linked policyholders of good asset
In addition, the choice of investment strategy will affect the security that the insurer is able to
offer to policyholders. It is important to ensure that guaranteed benefits are met, and – in the
case of with‐profits and unit‐linked business – that policyholders’ expectations are met.
In simple terms, the insurer will want high returns that are not volatile. However, these two
objectives conflict: investments offering higher returns normally show relatively volatile returns.
The insurer will attempt to balance returns and security, given the nature of its liabilities.
Before starting to consider the selection of the most appropriate investment strategy for a health
and care insurer, it will be useful to revisit briefly the characteristics of the major asset types. This
should be familiar from earlier subjects.
1 Asset characteristics: Asset types may be characterised by their risk and return. Other features include marketability,
1.1 Conventional government bonds: This refers to loan stock (bonds) issued and guaranteed by governments.
Conventional bonds offer a nominal return, which is guaranteed if held to redemption. (Note
however, that it is not guaranteed if it is sold prior to redemption, which is an important point
when it comes to consideration of matching issues.)
It is normally considered the most secure asset type, other than cash (depending on the
government issuing it). Although the market value of such bonds may fluctuate with the market,
the eventual redemption value will be unaffected by such fluctuations.
In most countries, it is the commonest and most marketable asset type. Dealing costs tend to be
1.2 Index\u2010linked government bonds: Index‐linked bonds differ from conventional (fixed‐interest bonds) in that the coupon payments
will be defined with reference to some index or value – such as local price inflation.
The amounts and variety of index‐linked bonds issued by a government are often smaller than the
amount of conventional bonds, which can render the index‐linked bonds slightly less marketable.
1.3 Corporate bonds: This refers to loan stock (bonds) issued and guaranteed by private companies.
1.4 Equities: Equity investment (shares) offers an income (the dividend) that is expected to increase in real
terms. The market value of the shares should also increase in real terms. As there is both growth
in income and capital value, the income yields on equities tend to be low (compared to bonds).
The income and capital value of shares can be highly volatile, which can be a problem if the
dividends are being used to meet liability outgo and/or the market value is being used to help
demonstrate solvency (or the share needs to be sold).
In many markets, equities can be highly marketable. In other markets, equity investment may not
really be an option because of the size and reliability of the local market, in which case the
investor might consider overseas investment in (more mature) overseas stock exchanges.
1.5 Property: Property investment might be direct (ie actually buying a property) or indirect (eg through shares
in a property company or a collective investment scheme that invests in property).
1.6 Cash: Cash usually refers to money held on overnight accounts earning spot rates of interest.
It is the most secure asset type, with the least variability in value. It is very liquid, and dealing
costs are almost non‐existent. However, the expected return on cash is relatively low.
These can be stated as follows: (a)       a company should select investments that are appropriate to the nature, term and
(b)       the investments should also be selected to maximise the overall return on the
assets, where overall return includes both investment income and capital gains
(c)       the extent to which (a) may be departed from in order to meet (b) will depend, among
other things, on the extent of the company’s free assets and the company’s appetite
These principles can be expressed also a:          the company should invest to maximise the overall return on the assets, subject to
the risk taken on being within the financial resources available to it.
This states the fundamental approach towards balancing risk and return. The aim is to maximise
return subject to an acceptable level of risk. The level of risk will depend on the financial
resources of the insurance company – ie its free assets, which is discussed in more details below.
3.1 Nature, term and currency of the l: The first of the three investment principles above is about reducing risk, and essentially concerns
the amount and timing of the income, or proceeds, arising from the assets. If it is possible to buy
assets whose income is identical in amount and timing to the outgo of money being paid out on
the liabilities, then the assets and liabilities would be perfectly matched and there would be no
Perfect matching is a theoretical concept that is usually neither achievable, nor desirable.
It eliminates all possibility of making: (ii)      When perfect matching may be desirable
Perfect or nearly perfect matching is desirable if the insurance company has very low free assets
such that, without matching, the probability of ruin would be unacceptably high.
However, the best starting point in determining an investment strategy is to consider the assets
that would best match the liabilities. In order to do this, it will be necessary to consider what
components make up the liability outgo and what the characteristics of these components are.
The liability outgo consists of: The expected liability outgo in any year, or month, depends on the monetary value of each
of the constituents and the probability of it being received or paid out.
The asset / liability modelling described later in the chapter will incorporate any probabilistic
effects. Here, it is sufficient to bear in mind that, in considering any particular contract, it should
be possible to estimate the likely future outgo in respect of uncertain elements such as claims,
Nature: The nature of each of the components of net liability outgo can be considered separately.
Benefit payments can be subdivided into: Guaranteed in monetary terms          Consisting of benefit payments where the amount
payable is specified in the insurance contract in
Guaranteed in terms of an index       Consisting of benefits whose amount is directly linked
of prices, earnings or similar        to such an index.
Indemnity                             Indicating policies such as private medical insurance
where the amount paid in respect of the policyholder is
dependent on the costs incurred in receiving treatment
Investment-linked                     Consisting of benefits where the amount is determined
directly by the value of the investments underlying the
\uf0b7 Guaranteed in monetary te:          Guaranteed in terms of an index – an IP benefit that is linked to price or earnings inflation.
\uf0b7 Investment\u2010linked \u: Expense payments tend to increase. The rate of increase is not strictly comparable to the
rate of change in a price (or earnings) index, but can be treated as such for investment
purposes. Hence they can be included with benefit payments guaranteed in terms of an
Expenses of an insurance company will consist largely of salaries, which should move in line with a
relevant earnings index. Other expenses will include property costs, computer costs, office
equipment etc, and these should move broadly in line with price inflation. In each case, the rate
of increase will not be strictly comparable as it will depend on the particular expenses of the
insurance company, which depend on its pay rise structure and its mix of other expenses,
however, the link to national indices should generally be adequate.
Premium payments are usually fixed in monetary terms (or may be linked to an index) and
hence can be thought of as negative benefit payments guaranteed in monetary terms (or in
terms of a prices index or similar).
As a result of the above, the liability outgo may be split into the same four categories as
shown in the table above, ie:
\uf0b7 guaranteed in monetary te:          guaranteed in terms of a prices index or similar (expenses and some benefits and
Term: As well as matching liabilities by nature, the insurance company will want to try to match by term
so that asset proceeds are available at the right times to meet liability outgo.
Currency: Liabilities denominated in a particular currency should be matched by assets in the same
currency, so as to reduce any currency risk.
An alternative way of matching liabilities denominated in other currencies is to invest in overseas
assets and then to hedge the currency risk by use of appropriate currency options.
An insurance company might also choose to invest in overseas assets to increase diversification,
invest in assets not available in the domestic currency and/or take advantage of attractive
overseas investments. If this leads to a currency mismatch, eg if the insurance company’s
liabilities are denominated in the domestic currency, then the insurance company can (again)
hedge the resulting currency risk using appropriate currency options.
3.2 Effects of the nature of liabiliti: This section considers the investment implications of the four liability types defined above.
Guaranteed in monetary terms: A company will ideally want to invest so as to ensure that it can meet the guarantees. This
means investing in assets that produce a flow of asset proceeds to match the liability outgo.
This will involve taking into account also the term of the liability outgo, and hence the
probability of the payments being made, so as to indicate the term of the corresponding
assets.
Recall that this form of liability applies to, for example, conventional CI policies. For these,
benefits (and often premiums) are fixed. With large numbers of policies, the cashflows from
benefits, expenses and premiums will be statistically predictable. So the insurance company
could try to invest in assets that have an equal (but opposite) cashflow.
Except for certain types of company it will probably be impossible in practice to find assets
whose proceeds exactly match the expected liability outgo. In particular, the terms of
available fixed-interest securities are often much shorter than the corresponding liabilities;
usually a best match is all that can be hoped for.
Furthermore, the proceeds from assets may exceed the net liability outgo. The excess will have to
be reinvested at unknown rates, making strict matching impossible. This issue can be overcome
by the use of zero‐coupon bonds, but they might not exist in the market being considered.
Even worse, it might be the case that for a block of recently written business, premium income
may exceed benefits and claims for some years, giving a net liability cash inflow in such years. No
asset type can match that (at least not without considering investing in derivatives), and so the
insurance company will have to invest these surplus premiums at unknown future rates.
Another problem is that liability outgo is rarely known with certainty. Therefore it will be
impossible to choose assets whose proceeds provide an exact match.
The technique of immunisation, covered in Subjects CM1 and CP1, may be used for this
purpose. However that technique is subject to theoretical and practical problems. A
technically superior (though more complicated) approach to assessing a possible best
match will be given in Section 4.
Immunisation is an alternative (really a second best) to exact matching. The aim is to protect the
investor from small changes in future interest rates. Immunisation is really only of relevance for
guaranteed liabilities, however, even then, the approach described later in the chapter will
normally be preferable.
Guaranteed in terms of a prices index or: A suitable match would be securities that are linked to the same index in which the
guarantee is denominated, if available, ideally chosen to match also the expected term of
the liability outgo. In their absence, a substitute would be assets that are expected to
Equities are not a perfect match for thi:          their return is not guaranteed, either in absolute or index‐linked terms
\uf0b7 there is a default risk a:          the return on equities is volatile, so the match in the short term is likely to be poor.
However:          the return does behave to some extent in line with price inflation, and so they offer a
reasonable match, particularly if a range of equities is chosen (to achieve diversification)
         over a longer period, the return tends to be less volatile, so the match will be better
         equities may be a better match for earnings‐related indices than price‐related indices.
Indemnity: The insurer will monitor the likely costs arising from the various treatments that are covered
by these medical insurance contracts. Normally, private medical insurance (and similar
products) are short-tail business with little scope for significant investment return.
This is because the premium income from all policies should be (broadly) sufficient to pay for the
claims of the unfortunate few, and to cover the insurer’s expenses. Therefore the reserves under
PMI policies will be very small, and so investment income on them will be negligible.
However, assets will still be required, so normally a mixture of cash and securities such as short‐
term fixed‐interest bonds would be suitable.
However, an insurer will occasionally need to establish reserves where it is aware of future
treatment that could persist over some future years. The amounts payable will be unknown
at the time of setting these provisions and any current costs will escalate in line with
medical inflation (often higher than price inflation) prior to final settlement.
Therefore, the assets recommended in these circumstances should be those that are
expected to provide a ‘real’ return over the relatively short period until the case goes off the
Investment\u2010linked: The benefits are guaranteed in the sense that their value can be determined at any time in
accordance with a definite formula based on the value of a specified fund of assets (or
investment index). Clearly the company could avoid any investment matching problems by
investing in the same assets as used to determine the benefits.
The most common example of this liability is a unit‐linked product. In some markets, the insurer
might be required to invest so as to match the underlying assets exactly. Even if not legally
obligatory, this is normal practice and there would have to be very strong reasons not to do so.
One such reason would be if the insurance company thought that it could profit from such a
deliberate mismatch (eg the units are defined in terms of Asset A and the insurance company
thinks that Asset B will perform better over the next 6 months so the assets invested by the
insurance company to meet the unit liabilities are switched from A to B).
3.3 Free assets: The existence of free assets in an insurance company means that it can depart from the
matching strategies outlined above so as to improve the overall return on its assets and
thereby benefit its policyholders, through lower premium rates, and its shareholders (if
It is almost always the case that assets with the highest expected return also have the
If the assets supporting guaranteed benefits were invested in the assets with the highest
expected return the probability that the asset proceeds will become inadequate may be too
high for comfort, ie the risk of insolvency is too great.
This assumes that only enough assets are held so that their expected proceeds will cover
the benefits. If there are free assets, they can be used as a cushion to reduce the
Example: Suppose a company has guaranteed fixed liabilities worth £100 now and it needs to choose some
appropriate investment strategy. In order to increase returns, it would like to move away from a
matched strategy and invest 50% in equities and the remaining 50% in government bonds.
Suppose the value of equities might fluctuate by  / 10% in the course of any year, and that
accounting regulations allow government bonds to be valued at book value if they are to be held
to maturity (ie downward market fluctuations on government bonds can be ignored). Also
assume that there is no minimum required solvency capital.
What investment strategy would be possible if the company possessed no free assets? If it were
to invest in £50 of equities, as it would like to, and £50 of bonds, then it would be likely that at
some point those equities fall in value to £45 and the total asset portfolio would then be worth
£95, making the company insolvent. In fact, with no free assets, the company cannot make any
equity investment.
What would be possible if the company had £7.50 of free assets? These free assets would enable
the company to withstand a drop in the value of the policyholders’ assets of £7.50 (if the free
assets themselves do not drop in value – in which case an allowance would be needed for that
movement). Thus the company could invest in up to £75 of equities. So the company can invest
the policyholders’ fund 50% in equities as originally hoped for.
The techniques discussed below can be used to determine how much of the free assets are
needed as a cushion so as to reduce the probability of insolvency to an acceptable level.
Clearly this use of the free assets is most appropriate in regard to the assets backing the
guaranteed benefits.
So, the greater the free assets, the greater the freedom in choosing assets for guaranteed
liabilities, ie the greater the variability of return the company can accept on the backing assets.
Also one could argue that it is a reasonable use of the free assets to mismatch investment-
linked benefits if by so doing the company can expect to achieve a higher return. Note
though that if this is done any return achieved above (or below) that on the ‘matched’ assets
will not accrue to the unit-linked policyholders but to the owners of the business.
A health insurer, with predominantly long‐term liabilities, holds a portfolio of equities. The
current market value of the portfolio is £100m, and the portfolio has recently been yielding an
overall dividend of 10%pa. What value would you place on this portfolio?
One method is to use a discounted cashflow method, by discounting both the value of expected
future dividends and the expected future sale values, at a rate of interest consistent with that
used for the valuation of the liabilities. A second is simply to use £100m as the asset value, on the
understanding that this value already reflects (in theory at least), expected future asset proceeds.
The best method to use depends on the purpose of the valuation, and the level of prudence
required.
3.4 Regulatory framework: The regulatory framework within a country may limit what a company can do in terms of
investment. The following controls may be implemented:
         restrictions on the types of assets in which an insurer can invest
Investment in assets perceived to be risky, such as equities, property and derivatives,
might be restricted (eg to x% of total funds or liabilities) or even forbidden.
         restrictions on the amount of any type of asset that can be considered for
For example, equities might only be allowed to count for 25% of assets in demonstrating
solvency. So an insurance company with, say, 40m in equities and 60m in government
bonds would only be able to show assets of 80m in its supervisory balance sheet.
         restrictions on the maximum exposure to a single counterparty / country
For example, there may be a restriction that shares in any one company may not amount
to more than 1% of the assets used to demonstrate solvency. This is to prevent over‐
         a requirement to hold a proportion of total assets in a particular class – for example
\uf0b7 a requirement to hold a m:          a limit on the extent to which mismatching is allowed at all
         a limit on the extent to which mismatching is allowed at all
\uf0b7 restrictions on the valua:          custodianship of assets eg restrictions on who is permitted to look after them.
Matching requirements: There may be a regulatory requirement to allow for mismatching. This could involve the
setting up of an investment mismatching reserve. The more a company decides to invest in
riskier assets with a higher expected return, the higher the resulting reserve might be. This
would increase the value of the liabilities and reduce the available free assets.
Other controls: The regulatory environment can also affect the choice of assets through their relationship
with the investment assumptions used to value the liabilities. A particular asset selection
may allow a company writing long-term health insurance products to use a higher
investment yield assumption and thereby reduce the value of the liabilities and increase the
free assets. However, such asset selections may not necessarily enable the company to
maximise the expected investment return.
For example, the rate of interest that can be used to value the liabilities might be higher if the
backing assets are government bonds than if they are equities.
Another regulatory constraint that might be important is the method required to value the assets.
For example, in countries where assets are valued at book value, there is a disincentive to invest
in equities because gains can only be seen when the assets are sold, and there will be times when
the company might not want to sell.
Another regulatory requirement is the need to hold solvency capital in addition to the supervisory
reserves and the question arises as to what should the solvency capital be invested in. The two
main options are:
         Since the solvency capital requirement might be seen as a strengthening of reserves
(eg instead of needing to hold assets to cover a reserve of $100,000, the company must
hold assets to cover a reserve of $100,000 and a solvency capital requirement of $5,000),
it can be argued that the statutory capital requirement should be invested in the same
way as the assets underlying the supervisory reserves for that business.
         The solvency capital requirement might be viewed as intrinsically different from the
related supervisory reserves, and so a different investment strategy would be adopted.
This would often be the case with investment‐linked business, where the assets in respect
of the solvency capital requirement might be invested in government bonds rather than
invested in the specific assets underlying the investment link.
4 Developing an appropriate investme: This section considers how an insurance company would use asset and liability modelling
techniques to assess the viability of any investment strategy. The starting point is to identify the
strategy that forms the most appropriate match for the liabilities. This will represent a ‘minimum
risk’ strategy for the insurance company. It then needs to consider what divergences from this
strategy will lead to greatest expected rewards to shareholders and/or policyholders, while
maintaining risk control commensurate with the its resources, particularly its available capital.
4.1 Using a model office to determine: Using a model of the business in force, a model investment portfolio can be built up using
the company’s proposed investment strategy and incorporating an appropriate proportion
The shareholders may not want to use all: deliberately mismatching the liabilities to increase return, and using free assets as a cushion,
means that those free assets might disappear to pay for any loss arising from the mismatch.
Instead, they might prefer to ‘save’ some of their free assets for other purposes, eg developing
new products, or financing new business strain.
What is appropriate here will be determined to some extent from the results of the
investigations below.
The liabilities and the assets would then be projected forward on assumptions that
represent expected future experience, although the company will want also to consider the
effect of variations from these.
In fact, one of the most important considerations will be how the proposed investment strategy
copes if there are significant market movements downwards.
For the assets, stochastic investment models can be incorporated in order to project future
investment income and changes in capital values. Inflation rate models can also be used to
project future expenses on the liabilities side.
As always in modelling work, great care will be required that the parameters are consistent,
ie that the relationships between the different variables should be suitably realistic. In particular,
for the assets, consistency will be needed between inflation, interest rates and market (capital)
values, and these should be consistent with the valuation interest rates used in the liability model.
The projected liabilities and assets can then be valued at the end of each year of the
projection on the company’s supervisory basis.
This implies that the insurance company wants to test solvency at the end of each year. With
modern computational power there should be no reason not to test this solvency continuously
throughout the projection period, to ensure that the insurance company is always solvent.
The item of interest will be the excess of the value of the assets over the value of the
liabilities. This will need to be sufficient to cover comfortably the level of solvency capital
required by the company, which may itself depend on the investment strategy being
investigated. What is ‘comfortable’ will depend on any regulatory requirements, the nature
of the business, and the level of cover provided in other companies.
An example of ‘comfortable’ cover could be having a solvency capital equal to twice the
minimum. But exactly how much cover is ‘comfortable’ will depend on whether the regulatory
authorities, perhaps ‘unofficially’, expect insurance companies to achieve a certain level of cover,
on the riskiness of the business in question and on what is normal in the market. (The market
norm is also a factor because brokers may examine the amount of cover that insurers show in
their published returns, perhaps via articles in the specialist financial press, and use it as their
main measure of an insurance company’s financial strength.)
Using a stochastic investment model and simulation techniques, the investigations can be
extended to produce a statistical distribution of the amounts available each year to cover
the level of solvency capital required. From this, the probability of potential future
insolvency can be estimated given a particular investment strategy.
The simulations might involve several hundreds or thousands of randomly generated ‘sample
paths’, each one representing the future solvency positions resulting from one set of randomly‐
generated investment returns. For example, a collection of sample paths might look like those
shown in Figure 1. The ratio of assets to liabilities at the date of projection is 1.2.
Time (in years): One of the paths clearly goes insolvent (assets less than liabilities). Others may denote
insolvency, depending on the extent of any required amount of minimum solvency capital under
In Figure 2 all of the sample paths go insolvent at some point, which would be highly worrying.
For valuing the liabilities the company’s current basis will most likely be chosen, but it
could incorporate into the simulation exercise dynamic assumptions that take into account
If the valuation basis is in any way contingent on the assets underlying the reserves, the return on
those assets, or even just the expected reinvestment yields in the market, then it is (again)
important to ensure consistency. In other words, the reserves in the model for any given year
should be calculated on a basis that reflects the simulated asset situation in that year as keeping
the valuation basis constant could give very misleading results.
It will also be very important to identify some measure of success (ie of profitability) that can be
used to compare investment strategies. Some aggregate measure of the insurance company’s
earned profit over a future time horizon would ideally be required. (An example for a proprietary
insurance company is given at the end of this sub‐section.)
Summarising the model office approach: 1)        allocate a certain amount of free assets to support the assets underlying the reserves
2)        perform asset‐liability model projections of the insurance company’s future assets (arising
from the starting assets defined above), and compare these total assets against the
3)        check that the excess of these assets over the liabilities exceeds any statutory solvency
capital requirement (or possibly, exceeds some comfy multiple of that requirement) for
the entire projection period for most (eg 99%) of the model runs
4) calculate some measure of aggr: 5)        repeat these last three steps assuming different (more or less risky) investment strategies
until the target probability of insolvency is achieved
6)        identify which of the possible strategies, having equal insolvency risk, produces the
Note that there are several potential we:          the issue of modelling risk – the conclusions of the asset‐liability modelling process will
only be valid to the extent that the model, and the inherent parameterisation, are valid
         the issue of considering only a portion of free assets – although the shareholders might
not want all of those assets to be put at risk in supporting an unmatched investment
strategy, that does not imply that the model should necessarily exclude certain assets
         the insurance company will need to be very careful if the statutory solvency capital
requirement is not constant, eg it may be defined as a function of the reserves. In this
case, an approach that is less vulnerable to error would be to include the solvency capital
requirement in the asset‐liability model to ensure that it is modelled correctly. (In other
words, a better approach might be for the model to be of the whole company but possibly
with some free assets earmarked ‘not for solvency demonstration’.)
The basic approach that has been described above is to take the level of free assets, decide on
some acceptable risk of insolvency (eg 1%), and then determine the most risky, and hence
rewarding, investment strategy compatible with those criteria. (Note that an insurance company
would never normally choose to increase risk without the possibility of a higher return. For
example, it might choose to hold inherently more risky assets (for which there is a higher
expected return), or mismatch its liabilities (opening up the possibility of increasing return), but it
would not choose to reduce diversification as this is a risk it could reduce without affecting
expected return.)
A minor variant of this is to take only a proportion of the free assets and determine the most
rewarding investment strategy feasible.
However, the simulations could also be used to determine the level of free assets that the
company needs in order to support a particular investment strategy and keep the
probability of insolvency below an acceptably low figure.
An alternative, which corresponds to what an insurance company might do in reviewing its
current strategy, is to take that investment strategy in conjunction with its actual free assets and
to estimate the probability of insolvency. This is the essence of dynamic solvency testing.
For a proprietary company, the process can be extended further and the effect of the
investment strategy on future shareholder earnings can be considered. In particular, an
investment strategy can be determined that maximises shareholder income whilst keeping
the risk of insolvency sufficiently low, bearing in mind the available level of free assets.
In the case of a proprietary company, profitability can be measured directly in terms of the value
of the distributions of profits made to the shareholders, ie as transfers from the projected income
statements (profit and loss accounts). This can therefore be used as a means of comparing the
merits of different possible investment strategies in conjunction with the appropriate risk
measure.
4.2 Fund manager assessment: Given that ‘The company should invest to maximise the overall return on the assets, subject
to the risk taken on being within the financial resources available to it’ as stated in Section
2, it is important that the actuary or other analyst should monitor the results of the fund
\uf0b7 the riskiness of the stra:          the performance of other fund managers with similar investment briefs.
Where possible, the assessment would be over several such periods to eliminate the effects
The fund manager could be an internal department, or may be a third party. For external
managers, an even more careful approach to monitoring should arguably be taken, since there
may be less control and it may be more difficult to access performance data.
4.3 Liquidity: If an insurer is writing products of a type that can produce widely fluctuating levels of
claims or if the insurer is in a state of run-down, it will be wise to maintain access to ready
For example, if the insurer has closed to new business and is merely managing the run‐off of the
existing business, the claims experience will become more volatile as the number of remaining
independent claims decreases. In this case, immediate cashflow could become a problem and so
a source of ready cash is advisable (or at least assets that can be realised at short notice, with
Where claims might fluctuate widely, it might be possible to reduce the resulting high liquidity
requirement by having appropriate reinsurance in place.
Even with reinsurance protection, the insurer will still be left with the obligation to pay the
gross claims perhaps far in advance of making recoveries from reinsurers. Note that this
may introduce tension with the matching requirements.
The matching requirements may have suggested that a longer‐term approach, such as investing in
equities and property, would normally be appropriate. However, even if the nature of the
liabilities have not changed in terms of term or underlying risk, the need for ready cash may mean
that shorter‐term investments would be a better idea.
4.4 Effect on product development and: The return on investments to support the liability outgo for any product will enable the
actuary to price (and design) more competitively. The company’s ability to invest more
widely than its competitors, due possibly to higher free assets, may result in a higher
overall investment return. This may enable the company to quote lower prices or to include
further features at the same price.
This is particularly the case for longer‐term contracts, where the investment return is likely to be
4.5 Asset valuation: When assessing an insurance company’s financial strength, putting a value on its liabilities is only
half the story. There are also different ways that the assets could be valued.
The method of valuation of assets will depend on the purpose of the exercise and the
For PMI business, as it has typically only short-term liabilities, there would be little
alternative but to value the backing assets (normally cash) at their market price.
For long-term liabilities, there are two: 1.        Discount the likely returns on these assets, including eventual disposal, on a basis
consistent with the valuation of liabilities.
2.        Use market values as an objective and readily-understood methodology. This
method has become the norm in many territories.
4.6 Treating customers fairly: It is unlikely with conventional health and care insurance products that the policyholders
have any expectation of investment gain through their insurances.
Nevertheless, the insurer will be wary of relying on investment returns to support the costs
of benefits to an excessive degree such that underperformance in this area would
necessitate a price increase at a policy review. This would almost certainly raise questions
In other words, policyholders of fixed benefit but reviewable premium products (eg reviewable CI
insurance), would not appreciate a rise in premium level purely as a result of under‐performance
of the insurer’s investments. (They may view the under‐performance as ‘not their problem’.)
The major exception to this would be investment‐linked products where the benefit (and/or
future premium levels) depends on the future investment performance.
The chapter summary starts on the next p: keep all the chapter summaries together for revision purposes.
Chapter 28 Summary: The investment strategy of a health and care insurer is of importance to its commercial
success, financial performance and security. The scale of importance depends on the
product and in particular on the size of the reserves.
The insurance company should invest so as to maximise the overall return on the assets,
subject to an acceptable level of risk.
Assets should be selected to match the nature, term and currency of the liabilities.
Liabilities guaranteed in monetary terms: Immunisation theory can be used to build up a suitable portfolio of government bonds, but
there are a number of problems and limitations with this approach.
Liabilities guaranteed in terms of some: The ideal is to invest in an asset based on the same index. An alternative is to invest in real
Indemnity liabilities: Short‐term assets are appropriate for most of these liabilities. There may be some longer‐
term liabilities that could be matched by real long‐term assets.
Investment\u2010linked liabilities: It is normal to invest in the assets underlying the investment link concerned.
Other considerations: The existence of free assets means that an insurer can depart from a matched position in
order to improve overall investment returns.
The regulator can also affect the assets:          restrictions on the types of assets in which an insurer can invest
         restrictions on the amount of any particular type of asset that can be considered for
         restrictions on the maximum exposure to a single counterparty / country
         a requirement to hold a certain proportion of total assets in a particular class – for
Overall strategy: The overall principle is to invest away from a matched position, to the maximum extent
possible, consistent with the desired level of risk, in order to maximise returns.
The insurance company will perform asset / liability modelling, to test the proposed
investment strategies, which could be used alternatively to investigate:
         the level of riskiness of investment strategy that can be supported,
         the level of free assets required to support any strategy, or
\uf0b7 the resulting probability: noting the interdependence between these three aspects.
The insurance company should keep a close eye on the performance of its fund managers.
It may need to keep a supply of ready funds, to allow for the volatility of its day‐to‐day
cashflows, particularly if modelling reveals that outcomes are very sensitive to experience.
There are various approaches to valuing assets. Two possibilities are a market value
approach and a discounted cashflow approach. The purpose of the valuation will have an
Health and care policyholders usually do not expect poor investment returns to influence the
premium reviews under reviewable contracts, unless maybe if the policies are unit‐linked.

27.2.label: Question 27.2
27.2.scenario:
A long‐term insurance company transacts regular premium deferred annuity contracts for both
   individuals and groups of employees. The contracts contain an optional waiver of premium rider
benefit to cover periods of sickness, for which an additional premium is charged. Currently, the
same premium rates and underwriting conditions apply to members of groups as to individuals.
Life cover is not available under the contracts.

The marketing manager has suggested that for group policies there should be no underwriting.
He also argues that, in view of the economies of scale available, the rider benefit should be
provided free of charge to groups.
27.2.question: Comment briefly on the marketing manager’s suggestions.

No underwriting for group policies: With no underwriting, there is a risk of anti‐selection because unhealthy individuals will be
The anti‐selection risk is lower with a group due to the ‘healthy worker’ effect, ...                         [½]
... but it is still present, primarily because the benefit is optional.                                       [½]
Also the risks are not independent from employee to employee and so there is a potential
... eg several employees contracting the same illness from a work‐related cause, or simply through
However, there should be greater homogeneity in the group than in a collection of individual
... which should lead to more predictable claims experience.                                                  [½]
Costs would be reduced if no underwriting is carried out.                                               [½]
The key point is whether this is more than offset by the greater claims cost that would be
Consideration should be given to the level of underwriting carried out by competitors offering
Offering the benefit for free: The economies of scale for group deferred annuity business are unlikely to pay for removing the
... although it might lead to a reduced cost compared with individual business, especially if free
If offering the free benefit were to boost sales significantly, it might pay for itself, by reducing the
per scheme cost of the fixed expenses.                                                                [½]
However, it would be risky to rely on this. Consideration should be given to how many schemes
currently have waiver of premium. This should give an indication of its popularity and hence
whether or not it is likely to increase sales.                                               [½]
There may be pressure from brokers to introduce it for business that is currently in force.             [½]
If a free waiver is also offered to business in force then the cost will be much higher.                [½]
Anti‐selection and heterogeneity are most likely in smaller groups.                                     [½]
If combined with no underwriting, the level of any free cover should be made dependent on group
... and might be zero for very small groups.                                                       [½]

(i).question: Why is it not appropriate to ask, ‘Do you have a dangerous job?’ on a proposal form?

Why it is not appropriate to ask \u2018D: In general, the questions asked must relate to facts that will be within the proposer’s knowledge.
Matters of opinion, or any questions that might require expert knowledge, medical or otherwise,
Whether or not a job is dangerous is a matter of opinion, and it might be argued that you need
some expert knowledge about health and safety at work to be able to provide an answer.

(ii).question: How could you identify those applicants with jobs that put them in a higher risk category?

How applicants with higher risk jobs may: It is perfectly legitimate to ask questions about the nature of a person’s job, but these must relate
to facts within the person’s knowledge.
So the insurer needs to decide what aspects of danger are important to it (eg working at heights,
working with dangerous chemicals) and ask directly about these aspects of the job.
\uf0b7 Do you work at unprotecte:          Does your job involve travelling by air as a non‐fare‐paying passenger on unscheduled
\uf0b7 Does your job involve man:          Does your job involve working offshore (such as oil / gas rig or ship) or travelling or

27.4.label: Question 27.4
27.4.scenario:
A proposal form for a stand‐alone critical illness insurance contract includes the following
   question:

‘Have any of your close relatives (parents, grandparents or siblings) suffered from heart disease,
stroke or cancer before the age of 60? If so, please give details.’

(i).question:
Explain the rationale for including this question in the proposal form.                       [2]

In a particular country, genetic tests have recently become available that can identify whether
someone has a high propensity of developing certain kinds of cancers.

The country’s government has, however, just made it illegal for any insurance company to make
use of such test results when underwriting applicants for health insurance. So anyone who has
had a genetic test prior to applying for a critical illness insurance policy does not have to disclose
to the insurer either the results of the test or even that they have had the test carried out.

Why ask about family history of disease?: Tendencies for these critical illnesses run, to an extent, in families. So the risk of incurring these
illnesses is partly inherited, ie genetically determined.                                            [½]
Applicants with a history of certain diseases in their close family therefore have a higher
probability of suffering these diseases themselves.                                                        [½]
Also, for most policies, it is only important to identify individuals who have a high risk of critical
... because late onset illnesses are far less likely to occur within the term of the policy and so are
unlikely to lead to a claim.                                                                         [½]
Therefore, by asking about family incidence of early‐onset diseases, the insurance company
should be able identify the individuals that are higher claim risks for a CI insurance policy.   [½]

(ii).question:
Explain how this might affect the insurance company, and discuss the actions it might take
in response to the ban.

Effect of the ban on the insurance compa: The result of the ban will be to increase the risk of anti‐selection against the insurance company.
Individuals who take genetic tests, and find they test positive for an increased risk of developing
cancer, will be more likely to seek CI insurance that covers the cancer as a consequence of the
This will increase the proportion of higher risk individuals applying for insurance.                       [½]
As these people are not required to give any indication of having had a test, the insurer will offer
them standard terms, ie standard premium rates and no exclusions (assuming there are no other
legitimate indicators of the increased risk).                                                      [½]
If no changes are made, the claims experience under the insurance company’s standard risks will
worsen, producing losses on the business.                                                   [½]
Possible actions: 1.        Raise premium rates across the standard rates class.                                          [½]
This would be effective in turning the class back to profitability, provided the genetic test
For example, if the test indicated that an individual would almost definitely develop the
condition, and the condition was common, then the rate rise would have to be so large as
to make the insurance unworkable (no normal – untested – person would ever apply). [½]
2.        The diseases identified by genetic tests could be excluded from the list of valid claims for
This will be most effective for early‐onset conditions that are very reliably predicted by
However, applying exclusions for all (or a large proportion) of the diseases that might be
implicated by genetic test results, would lead to so many exclusions that no‐one would
3.        If all else fails, the insurer would have to stop writing the business.                  [½]

(i).question: Define pre‐authorisation, as applied to PMI products.

Pre\u2010authorisation for PMI: Pre‐authorisation is the procedure whereby claims are authenticated against the policy conditions
and medical need established and agreed, prior to treatment.                                  [½]
The procedure also directs the claimant to use the most suitable treatment provider, and
authorises the treatment provider to proceed with the treatment.                                        [½]

(ii).question:
Describe the concept of disability counselling for IP insurance products, and its
advantages for both policyholders and insurers.

Disability counselling for IP insurance: Disability counselling is the process whereby an insurer’s representative visits a new insurance
The policyholder can be given advice on how to deal with their disability or illness.                   [½]
The policyholder can be reminded about the benefits payable (dates, amounts and conditions). [½]
Details of State welfare payments and local assistance services can also be provided.                   [½]
The insurer will be able to establish the severity of the illness and (if applicable) be able to
estimate a likely date for return to work.                                                              [½]
The representative can also promote the idea of returning to work as soon as is practically
possible, keeping the insurer’s costs to a minimum.                                                     [½]
The advantages to the policyholder (above) should make the product more attractive and thus
give the insurer a marketing benefit.                                                       [½]

27.6.label: Question 27.6
27.6.scenario:
An insurance company is considering entry to the group risks market for a wide range of health

and care contracts.

(i).question:
Describe how medical underwriters seek to manage risk for a UK‐type private medical
insurance policy.

Medical underwriting can be used to mana:          It can protect an insurance company from anti‐selection and in particular from individuals
whose health is so seriously impaired that it is impossible to assess the risk accurately. [½]
For PMI, anti‐selection can lead to both higher than expected frequency of claims and
         The medical underwriting process enables insurers to identify individuals with a
substandard health risk for whom special terms must be quoted.                                    [½]
An insurer may, however, aim to write a large proportion of the business that it accepts at
         For the substandard risks, the medical underwriting process will identify the most suitable
... and premium level for the special terms to be offered.                                        [½]
         Adequate risk classification within the underwriting process will help to ensure that all
         Medical underwriting will help in ensuring that actual morbidity experience does not
depart too far from that assumed in the pricing of the contracts being sold.           [½]

(ii).question:
Describe the sources of medical information typically used in underwriting such a private
medical insurance policy.

Medical evidence can be obtained from th:          questions on the proposal form completed by the applicant                                         [½]
         reports from medical doctors that the applicant has consulted                                     [½]
         a medical examination carried out on the applicant at the request of the insurer                  [½]
         specialist medical tests on the applicant.                                                      [½]

27.7.label: Question 27.7
27.7.question: What different forms could the special terms for a PMI policy take?

Possible special terms for PMI:          exclude claims for specified treatments (effectively reducing the benefit to zero)
         charge a higher premium, eg 120% of the standard premium, (ie a 20% loading)
         impose an increased policy excess for particular conditions / treatments (effectively
reducing the benefit payable for those conditions)
         defer the decision to provide cover if the period of risk is deemed temporary
         offer a slightly different type of policy, eg a budget version of PMI or a health cash plan
         decline cover if the additional risk is thought to be too high.
Each of these options could be used separately or a combination may be applied.

(i).question:
Describe four ways in which a health and care insurance company can obtain

underwriting information about a person making an application for a health insurance
contract.

\uf0b7 income:          whether special terms have been applied (or offered) by other insurers in the past.
This is a confidential report written by the applicant’s medical attendant (doctor). It gives an
account of the applicant’s medical history and an opinion about the applicant’s future health. [½]
The applicant is required to undergo a medical examination by the insurer’s medical officer or an
independent doctor. This may include routine medical tests, eg blood pressure tests.            [½]
This will involve investigations about specific conditions, eg an electrocardiogram, chest X‐ray etc.

(ii).question:
Explain how the information in part (i) would be used by the insurance company, and the
purpose that is served by this process.

Use of the information: The information is used to determine whether the applicant is insurable at standard rates …         [½]
… and in the case of the latter, what the special terms should be.                                  [½]
Special terms include:          postponing the decision (if the period of higher risk is deemed temporary)                         [½]
         declining insurance, if allowed in the country concerned                                           [½]
         offering a different type of policy (ie a less risk intensive one)                                 [½]
         issuing a contract with exclusion from particular causes of claim                                  [½]
Purpose of underwriting: The aim is to ensure that the policyholder is charged a premium which accurately reflects the
... and that as few applicants as possible are declined insurance.                                           [½]
Underwriting will help to ensure that the actual experience does not depart too far from the
assumptions of the pricing basis, and hence that the expected profitability will materialise.                [½]
Underwriting will help reduce the financial risk from over‐insurance (preventing people from
taking out policies for larger sums insured than they need).                                                 [½]
The intended result is to minimise the risk of anti‐selection by high‐risk applicants seeking

(iii).question:
Explain why all four sources of information are not necessarily obtained for all applicants,
and how the company may decide upon which of the four to obtain in any particular case.

Why not all information for everyone?: In many cases, the risk can be adequately and accurately classified on less than the total possible
... and this therefore saves on expense.                                                                     [½]
The majority of applicants can be judged to be a standard risk from the proposal form. The few
high risks that are let through at inadequate premiums can be tolerated by the insurer provided
their levels of benefit are not too high.                                                      [½]
Determining which information to obtain: All applicants will fill in the proposal form.                                                               [½]
Adverse information given on the proposal form will automatically trigger either or both of the
Benefit levels higher than the company’s chosen threshold for a particular age and contract type
will automatically lead to PMAR and/or medical examination even if proposal form is clear.     [½]
As a medical examination is more expensive the automatic threshold is higher for the medical
Specialist examinations will be triggered when necessary to provide additional clarification about
conditions identified from any of the other sources.                                            [½]

27.9.label: Question 27.9
27.9.scenario:
You are an actuary working for a large well‐established health insurance company. It specialises
in, and is a leading provider of, individual critical illness and pre‐funded LTCI business in a

developed country. Its business is sold mainly through insurance intermediaries.

The Government of the country is about to change the regulations governing health insurance.
Insurers will no longer be allowed to use family medical history when making underwriting
decisions.
27.9.question: Outline the possible implications of this change for the insurance company.

Solution: Family medical history is relevant to risk classification because the propensity for certain diseases
is known to be partially inherited.                                                                [½]
The insurer will therefore have less information on which to base its underwriting decisions, and
so the assessment of the risk associated with each applicant will be less accurate.             [½]
Hence, the insurer may accept a greater proportion of substandard risks on normal underwriting
terms, leading to anti‐selection and an increase in claims.                                  [½]
Of particular concern will be applicants who are aware of a family history of a particular
hereditary medical condition that would previously have led to the policy being declined (or at
least heavily rated).                                                                           [1]
On the other hand, the insurer may strengthen its underwriting standards in other areas, and so
reject (or rate up) applicants who would otherwise be acceptable risks, and so reject good
business.                                                                                      [1]
The insurer may need to increase its premiums to allow for the anti‐selection, resulting in reduced
sales (particularly from better risks).                                                          [½]
In addition, margins may need to be increased (eg in pricing or reserving), to reflect the reduced
risk assessment.                                                                                 [½]
There may be a problem of lapse and re‐entry, whereby policyholders who have had premiums
rated on account of their family history, might reapply for insurance and be accepted on normal
rates.                                                                                        [½]
This may cause the insurer to incur additional costs and reduce its future profits.                  [½]
The impact of the change may be greater for this insurer relative to others because its two main
products (CI insurance and pre‐funded LTCI) are those where claim propensity may be most
affected by family history.                                                                    [½]
By not asking for family history information, this should make the product easier to sell (eg fewer
questions on the application form).                                                              [½]
If the insurance company uses less information in making its underwriting decision, faster
underwriting decisions could be made.                                                                [½]
These factors should make the products more attractive to potential customers and sellers, and
so lead to increased sales.                                                                   [½]
However, since this change will affect all insurers, the insurer’s market share may be unaffected
(all other things being equal).                                                                 [½]
However, up to now, this ‘large well‐established’ insurer may have been able to make better
underwriting decisions using family history compared to its competitors, based on its greater
experience. The insurer will no longer have a competitive advantage in this respect.                   [1]
Underwriting costs could also be reduced, eg there will be less information to process.                [½]
On the other hand, if the insurance company decides to increase its use of other underwriting
methods, eg use of medical examinations, this may offset any sales or expense advantages.     [1]
Sales procedures, marketing literature and application forms will need to be redesigned to
accommodate the change.                                                                                [½]
There will be less scope for claims being declined due to non‐disclosure (because there is less
information that needs to be disclosed).                                                        [½]
Any reinsurance terms will need to be renegotiated following the change to underwriting
procedures.                                                                               [½]
[Maximum 9]
